5: kî gāḏôl ʾelōhênû mikkol hāʾelōhîm — ‘for our God is great above all gods.’ Solomon’s letter to Huram of Tyre contains an embedded incomparability-claim: YHWH surpasses all other deities. The comparative mikkol hāʾelōhîm (above all gods) does not deny the existence of other deities but asserts YHWH’s incomparable superiority — an OT pattern found in Exod 18:11 (Jethro), Ps 77:13, Ps 95:3. The Chronicler’s version of Solomon’s letter to Huram is significantly different from 1 Kgs 5:2-6 — the Chronicler inserts this theological declaration about YHWH’s greatness, absent in Kings. The insertion reflects the Chronicler’s concern to present the temple-building as theologically grounded in YHWH’s incomparability, not merely as a political-architectural project.
6: ûmî ʾănî ʾăšer ʾebneh lô bayit — ‘And who am I that I should build him a house?’ The humility-question embedded in Solomon’s embassy to Huram. The verse continues: ‘since heaven and the highest heaven (wᵉhāšāmayim šᵉmê haššāmayim) cannot contain him.’ The term šᵉmê haššāmayim (heaven of heavens, the highest heaven) is the superlative of transcendence: not even the infinite expanse of the heavens contains YHWH. The parallel passage is 1 Kgs 8:27 (Solomon’s prayer at the temple dedication); the Chronicler moves the theological observation from the dedication-prayer to the preliminary embassy, making the architectural humility the premise of the project rather than its conclusion. Stephen cites this theological tradition in Acts 7:48-49 (quoting Isa 66:1-2): ‘the Most High does not dwell in houses made by hands.’ The temple’s paradox — a house for the One no house can contain — is embedded in the founding documents of the project.
14: yōdēaʿ laʿăśôt — ‘skilled to work / knowing how to make.’ Huram’s description of his master craftsman uses the participial form of yāḏaʿ (to know) + infinitive of ʿāśāh (to do, make) — ‘knowing-to-make,’ i.e., skilled in making. This technical-skill usage of yāḏaʿ (know) is the same as Exod 31:3-5, where Bezalel is filled with the Spirit of God ‘in ḥokmāh and tᵉḇûnāh and daʿat and in all craftsmanship.’ The wisdom/knowledge vocabulary spans intellectual discernment and technical craft — Hebrew does not divide the epistemological from the artisanal. The MKT translates ‘skilled to work,’ preferring the craft-competency sense over a purely cognitive rendering of yāḏaʿ. The craftsman’s materials span gold, silver, bronze, iron, stone, wood, purple, blue, fine linen, crimson — the full range that Bezalel’s tabernacle-craftsmanship also covered (Exod 35:35).
1: bayit lᵉšēm YHWH — ‘a house for the name of the LORD.’ The šēm-theology (name-theology) is fundamental to the Deuteronomic and Chronistic understanding of the temple: YHWH does not literally dwell in the temple as in a house but causes his šēm (name, representing his presence and claim) to dwell there. The distinction — presence-by-name rather than local confinement — guards against the ancient Near Eastern notion that a temple deity is trapped, manipulable, or dependent on its house. When the temple is destroyed, YHWH is not displaced; his name was placed there by covenant, not by necessity. The phrase bayit lᵉšēm YHWH appears in Deut 12:5,11,21 as the standard formulation for the centralization of worship at the one chosen place. The MKT maintains ‘a house for the name of the LORD,’ preserving the name-theology rather than softening it to ‘for the LORD.’
2: šibʿîm ʾelep nōśᵉʾê massāʾ ûšᵉmōnîm ʾelep ḥōṣᵉḇ bāhār — ‘seventy thousand burden-bearers, eighty thousand stonecutters in the hill country.’ The scale of the conscription — 150,000 laborers plus 3,600 supervisors (v. 18) — reflects the enormous logistical operation the temple-building required. The parallel account in 1 Kgs 5:13-18 uses the term mas (corvée labor force) for the thirty-thousand Israelites. The Chronicler’s account specifies that only resident aliens (gēr) were conscripted for the forced labor (2:17), not Israelites — a theological correction that protects Israel’s free status (consistent with the Exodus memory: Israel was not to return to slave-labor conditions).
3: kaʾăšer ʿāśîtā ʿim-dāwîḏ ʾābî — ‘Deal with me as you dealt with my father David.’ The diplomatic appeal to prior precedent (kaʾăšer, as/just as) is standard ancient Near Eastern treaty language: Solomon frames his request within the established relationship between the Hiram-David alliance. The cedar-wood agreement for the ark-tent (1 Chr 14:1; 2 Sam 5:11) becomes the basis for the much larger temple-building request. By invoking David’s precedent, Solomon signals continuity: this is not a new political alignment but the deepening of an existing covenantal business relationship.
4: liśrōp qṭōret besāmîm wᵉlᵉmaʿăreket leḥem tāmîḏ ûlᵉʿōlôt bōqer wāʿereḇ — ‘for burning fragrant incense, for the continual bread of the Presence (leḥem tāmîḏ), and for burnt offerings morning and evening.’ The three-fold liturgical purpose of the temple — incense, bread of the Presence, daily burnt offerings — enumerates the permanent cultic activities. tāmîḏ (continual, perpetual) is the technical term for the never-ceasing sanctuary services: the incense burned perpetually, the bread was perpetually before YHWH, the daily offerings were perpetually maintained. These perpetual rites are the backbone of Israel’s covenant relationship; the temple is not primarily an occasional gathering place but the permanent infrastructure of continual worship. The NT applies the tāmîḏ principle to Christ’s intercession: ‘he always lives to make intercession’ (Heb 7:25) — the perpetual mediation the temple enacted is fulfilled in the eternal high priest.
7: šelaḥ-lî ʾîš ḥākām laʿăśôt bᵉzāhāḇ ûḇkesep ûḇneḥōšet ûḇḇarzel — ‘Now send me a craftsman skilled in working with gold, silver, bronze, and iron.’ Solomon’s request for a skilled craftsman echoes Moses’s Bezalel-appointment in Exod 31:2-5, where Bezalel is filled with the Spirit of God in ḥokmāh (wisdom), tᵉḇûnāh (understanding), and daʿat (knowledge) ‘in all craftsmanship’ — including gold, silver, bronze. The parallel signals that Solomon’s temple-building stands in the Bezalel-craftsmanship tradition: the same multi-material expertise that produced the tabernacle now produces the permanent temple. The addition of iron (barzel) beyond the tabernacle materials reflects the Iron Age technological context of Solomon’s era.
8: wᵉʾarzê haallᵉḇānôn wᵉḇᵉrôšîm wᵉʾalgûmmîm — ‘cedar, cypress, and algum timber from Lebanon.’ Lebanon cedar (erez) is the prestige timber of the ancient Near East — durable, fragrant, massive, inaccessible except by special arrangement with the Phoenician coastal kingdoms. bᵉrôšîm (cypress, or juniper) is the standard secondary timber for construction alongside cedar. ʾalgûm (algum wood) is likely sandalwood from Ophir or India — an exotic luxury timber whose precise identification is debated; it appears elsewhere only in 1 Kgs 10:11-12 (where it is almug, probably the same wood). The triple timber specification signals that the Solomonic temple mobilized the full range of Levantine luxury construction materials, not merely local resources.
9: kî habbayit ʾăšer ʾănî bôneh gāḏôl wᵉniplāʾ — ‘for the house I am going to build must be magnificent and wonderful.’ gāḏôl (great) and niplāʾ (wonderful, marvelous) — the second adjective is the participle of pālāʾ, the root of wonders and miracles (Exod 3:20; 15:11; Ps 77:14: ‘the God who works wonders’). By calling the planned building niplāʾ, Solomon frames the temple as itself a miraculous work — not merely impressive architecture but a wonder in the category of YHWH’s mighty acts. The temple’s grandeur participates in the same semantic field as the Exodus and creation; it is YHWH’s own wonder in material form.
10: wᵉhinnēh laḥōṣᵉḇîm lᵉkōrᵉtê hāʿēṣîm nātatî ḥittîm ûśᵉʿōrîm wāzayit wāyyayîn — ‘For your servants who fell the timber, I will provide: wheat, barley, olive oil, and wine.’ The provision-list for Huram’s workers (twenty thousand cors each of wheat and barley, twenty thousand baths each of olive oil and wine) represents a massive food-security commitment. The kōr (cor) is the largest dry measure (approximately 220 liters); twenty thousand cors of wheat is roughly 4.4 million liters — the annual food supply for tens of thousands of workers. The exchange structure (timber for provisions) establishes the temple as a political-economic project that draws the resources of the entire Israelite economy into its construction.
11: beʾahabat YHWH ʾet-ʿammô nᵉtānᵉkā ʿălêhem melek — ‘Because the LORD loves his people, he has made you their king.’ Huram’s letter contains a remarkable theological statement: a Phoenician king attributes Solomon’s kingship to YHWH’s love for Israel. beʾahabat YHWH ʾet-ʿammô (in the love of YHWH for his people) — Huram’s recognition of YHWH’s covenantal love for Israel represents the Chronicler’s vision of how surrounding nations are meant to perceive Israel: as a people under divine love, whose king is appointed by a God who is loyal to them. This is the missional edge of Deuteronomy 4:6-8 — nations observing Israel’s covenant order and drawing theological conclusions about YHWH.
12: bārûk YHWH ʾelōhê yiśrāʾēl ʿōśēh haššāmayim wᵉʾet-hāʾāreṣ — ‘Praised be the LORD, the God of Israel, maker of heaven and earth.’ Huram’s doxology — addressed to YHWH as creator of heaven and earth — goes beyond diplomatic courtesy. The formula ʿōśēh haššāmayim wᵉʾet-hāʾāreṣ (maker of heaven and earth) is the standard creation-doxology of the psalter (Ps 115:15; 121:2; 124:8; 134:3; 146:6) and appears in Gen 14:19 (Melchizedek’s blessing). Huram uses the same confessional vocabulary that Israel’s own liturgy employed. The Chronicler places this doxology in the mouth of a foreign king to show that YHWH’s character as Creator is recognizable even beyond Israel’s covenant boundaries.
13: wᵉʿattāh šālaḥtî ʾîš ḥākām yôdēaʿ bînāh ḥûrām ʾābî — ‘I am sending you a skilled craftsman of great ability: Huram-abi.’ ḥākām yôdēaʿ bînāh — three wisdom-cognates stacked: ḥākām (wise, skilled), yōḏēaʿ (knowing), bînāh (understanding, discernment). The triple wisdom-vocabulary places Huram-abi in the Bezalel-tradition (Exod 31:3: ‘filled with the Spirit of God, with wisdom (ḥokmāh), understanding (tᵉḇûnāh), and knowledge (daʿat)’). The Chronicler’s name ḥûrām ʾābî (Huram my father) rather than 1 Kgs 7:13’s simply ‘Hiram’ may be an honorific — ‘my master craftsman’ — or may reflect a different textual tradition about the craftsman’s name.
15: wᵉʿattāh hahittîm wᵉhaśᵉʿōrîm haššemen wᵉhayyayin ʾăšer ʾāmar ʾăḏōnî yišlaḥ laʿăḇāḏāyw — ‘Now let my lord send to his servants the wheat, barley, olive oil, and wine that my lord promised.’ Huram’s reply confirms the provisions-for-timber agreement and references the annualized grain-and-oil payment promised in v. 10. The term ʾāḏōnî (my lord) — used by Huram for Solomon — reflects the diplomatic register of ancient Near Eastern correspondence, where the junior party addresses the senior party as ‘lord.’ The commercial and diplomatic framing does not diminish the theological significance: international commerce serves the temple project, and pagan kings use honorifics before the covenant king as an inadvertent enactment of Isa 60:3’s vision (‘nations shall come to your light, and kings to the brightness of your rising’).
16: wᵉʾănaḥnû niḵrōt ʿēṣîm min-haallᵉḇānôn kᵉkol-ṣōrᵉkā wᵉhēḇēʾnū ʾōtām rāpôt ʿal-hayyām yāpô — ‘We will cut as much timber from Lebanon as you need and float it to you in rafts by sea to Joppa.’ The timber-flotation route — cut in Lebanon, floated as sea-rafts (rāpôt) to Joppa, then transported overland to Jerusalem — is a logistical detail consistent with known Phoenician maritime timber trade. Joppa (modern Jaffa) is the natural Mediterranean port for Jerusalem-bound goods, approximately 55 km to the west. The route confirms the historical plausibility of the Solomon-Hiram timber treaty; the same route is referenced in Ezra 3:7 for the post-exilic temple reconstruction, establishing a consistent Joppa-Jerusalem timber corridor across centuries.
17: wayyispōr šᵉlōmōh kol-hāʾanāšîm haggērîm — ‘Solomon took a census of all the resident aliens (gēr) in the land.’ gēr (resident alien, sojourner) is the legal category for non-Israelites living within Israelite territory. The Chronicler specifies that only gērîm — not Israelites — were conscripted for the temple labor. This distinction guards the memory of Exodus: Israel was once the gēr in Egypt; now Canaanite gērîm serve the building project under the covenant people. The gēr’s labor for the sanctuary echoes the Mosaic provision that resident aliens were incorporated into Israel’s covenant community with obligations and protections (Lev 19:33-34; Deut 24:14-15).
18: wayyāśem mēhem šibʿîm ʾelep nōśᵉʾê massāʾ — ‘He assigned seventy thousand as laborers, eighty thousand as quarrymen in the hills, and thirty-six hundred as supervisors to make them work.’ The supervisory ratio — 3,600 overseers for 150,000 workers = roughly 1:42 — is administratively detailed. The term nōśᵉʾê massāʾ (burden-bearers) describes the lowest tier of manual labor: carrying quarried stone, timber, and other materials. The three-tier hierarchy (laborers, quarrymen, supervisors) mirrors the organizational structure of major ancient Near Eastern building projects documented in Assyrian and Egyptian records. The Chronicler’s detail serves his consistent interest in proper order and administration as expressions of covenant faithfulness.
